questions.q: A local FBLA chapter decides to sell its clubhouse. Which factor would most influence the selling price?
options: Number of years the club has owned the building
Market conditions in the surrounding neighborhood
Number of members in the club
questions.explain: Market conditions heavily affect what buyers are willing to pay because real estate value depends on local supply, demand, and comparable sales.
optionExplanations: Years of ownership do not directly set market value.
Correct. Neighborhood market conditions strongly influence sale price.
Membership count is unrelated to the property's market value.
Prior ownership history usually matters far less than current market conditions.

questions.q: When a property increases in value due to a new public transit stop nearby, this is an example of:
questions.explain: Appreciation is an increase in property value, often caused by favorable external changes such as improved transportation access.
optionExplanations: Depreciation is a decline in value over time.
Obsolescence refers to a loss in value due to outdated features or external negatives.
Correct. Appreciation means the property's value rises.
Capitalization is a valuation concept, not the increase itself.

questions.q: In the real estate industry, a "fixture" refers to:
options: Personal property not attached to the home
Anything nailed down but easily removable
An item permanently attached to the property
questions.explain: A fixture is personal property that has become real property because it is permanently attached to the land or building.
optionExplanations: Items not attached remain personal property.
Ease of removal is not the main legal test once an item is permanently attached.
Correct. Permanent attachment makes an item a fixture.
Seasonal decor is normally personal property.

questions.q: Which of the following is considered personal property rather than real property?
options: A chandelier wired into the ceiling
questions.explain: A freestanding refrigerator is movable and not permanently attached, so it is personal property rather than real property.
optionExplanations: Built-in appliances are usually fixtures.
A wired chandelier is attached and treated as a fixture.
Correct. A freestanding refrigerator is personal property.
Wall-to-wall carpeting is typically treated as attached.

questions.q: What is the term for land and everything permanently attached to it?
questions.explain: Real estate includes the land plus permanent improvements and attachments such as buildings and fixtures.
optionExplanations: Personal property is movable and not attached to the land.
Correct. Real estate is land and permanent attachments.
Tangible assets is broader and not the specific real estate term.
Fixtures are only one component of real estate, not the full term.

questions.q: A buyer interested in a home near a school is using which principle of value?
questions.explain: Anticipation means value is influenced by expected future benefits, such as convenience or access to desired amenities like schools.
optionExplanations: Substitution compares a property to similar alternatives.
Correct. Anticipation involves expected future benefits.
Progression concerns lower-valued properties benefiting from higher-valued nearby properties.
Conformity involves similarity within a neighborhood.

questions.q: If a homeowner paints their home bright pink in a neighborhood of neutral-colored homes, which principle might negatively affect their home's value?
questions.explain: Regression occurs when a better or more distinctive property is surrounded by lesser or incompatible properties, which can reduce its value in that setting.
optionExplanations: Correct. Regression can occur when a property does not fit surrounding properties and value is pulled down.
Contribution measures how much a feature adds to value.
Supply and demand is broader and not the specific principle being tested here.
Increasing returns is not the standard appraisal principle that fits this situation.

questions.q: Which professional would be most involved in analyzing demographic trends for property development?
questions.explain: Urban planners study population patterns, land use, and community development needs to guide future development decisions.
optionExplanations: Appraisers estimate current value rather than long-range demographic planning.
Correct. Urban planners analyze demographic and land-use trends.
Loan officers focus on mortgage qualification.
Inspectors evaluate physical condition, not demographic trends.

questions.q: A property owner builds an additional garage to attract buyers. This is an example of:
questions.explain: An added garage may be an over-improvement if the cost of the improvement exceeds the amount the market is willing to pay for it.
optionExplanations: Correct. Adding an amenity beyond what the market supports can be an over-improvement.
Highest and best use refers to the most profitable legal use of land.
Capital gain is profit realized on sale.
Equity growth is the owner's increased value stake, not the improvement itself.

questions.q: The right to occupy and use property is known as:
questions.explain: Possession is the legal right to occupy and control property.
optionExplanations: Correct. Possession means the right to occupy and use the property.
Title refers to legal ownership evidence.
Equity is the owner's financial interest in the property.
Conveyance is the transfer of ownership rights.

questions.q: If a property's location is its most valuable asset, which factor is at play?
questions.explain: Situs refers to the economic preference for a location, which can significantly influence property value.
optionExplanations: Scarcity means limited supply.
Correct. Situs means the value created by location.
Conformity concerns neighborhood similarity.
Substitution compares one property to available alternatives.

questions.q: Which of the following is an economic characteristic of land?
questions.explain: Situs is considered an economic characteristic because it reflects the desirability of a location in the marketplace.
optionExplanations: Immobility is a physical characteristic of land.
Indestructibility is also a physical characteristic.
Correct. Situs is an economic characteristic tied to location preference.
Non-homogeneity describes uniqueness, typically treated as a physical characteristic.

questions.q: When a property owner fails to maintain the property, leading to value loss, this is called:
questions.explain: Physical deterioration is loss in value caused by wear, damage, or neglect of the property's physical condition.
optionExplanations: Functional obsolescence involves outdated design or layout.
Correct. Poor upkeep causes physical deterioration.
Economic obsolescence comes from outside influences.
Deferred maintenance is postponed repair, but the value loss itself is categorized as physical deterioration.

questions.q: The process of transferring ownership from one person to another is called:
questions.explain: Conveyance is the legal act of transferring title to real property from one party to another.
optionExplanations: Leasing transfers temporary possession, not ownership.
Correct. Conveyance means transfer of ownership.
A mortgage creates a lien to secure a loan.
Tenancy refers to a form of possession or occupancy.

questions.q: A zoning law that prohibits commercial buildings in a residential neighborhood is an example of:
questions.explain: Zoning regulations are enacted under the government's police power to protect public health, safety, and welfare.
optionExplanations: Eminent domain is the power to take property for public use with compensation.
Building codes regulate construction standards, not land-use separation itself.
Correct. Zoning is an exercise of police power.
Adverse possession is a method of acquiring title through open, hostile possession.

questions.q: If a city requires a property for a new road and compensates the owner, this is known as:
questions.explain: Eminent domain allows government to take private property for public use, and condemnation is the legal process used with compensation.
optionExplanations: Correct. Government acquisition for a road with compensation is condemnation under eminent domain.
Escheat transfers property to the state when an owner dies without heirs.
Foreclosure follows mortgage default.
Adverse possession involves private possession over time, not government taking.

questions.q: A tenant staying after their lease has expired without the landlord's consent is in:
questions.explain: Tenancy at sufferance occurs when a tenant remains after the lease ends without the landlord's approval.
optionExplanations: Tenancy for years has a definite end date agreed in advance.
Tenancy at will continues only with mutual consent.
Correct. Holding over without consent is tenancy at sufferance.
Periodic tenancy renews automatically for successive periods.

questions.q: Which deed provides the greatest protection to the buyer?
questions.explain: A general warranty deed provides the broadest warranties, covering title defects from both current and prior owners.
optionExplanations: A quitclaim deed gives no warranties of title.
A bargain and sale deed provides limited protection.
Correct. A general warranty deed offers the strongest buyer protection.
A special warranty deed only covers the grantor's period of ownership.

questions.q: If a neighbor builds a fence two feet over the property line, this is called:
questions.explain: An encroachment occurs when a structure or improvement unlawfully extends onto another person's land.
optionExplanations: An easement is a legal right to use another's land for a purpose.
Correct. Crossing a property line with a fence is an encroachment.
A lien is a claim against property to secure a debt.
A covenant is a restriction or promise affecting land use.

questions.q: Which lien takes the highest priority in payment?
questions.explain: Property tax liens generally have priority over other liens because taxes fund local government obligations and are given statutory superiority.
optionExplanations: Mortgage liens are usually junior to tax liens.
Correct. Property tax liens are commonly first in priority.
Mechanic's liens do not usually outrank property tax liens.
Judgment liens are typically lower in priority.

questions.q: A restrictive covenant in a subdivision might:
options: Limit the size of homes built
questions.explain: Restrictive covenants are private deed restrictions that can control matters such as size, appearance, or permitted uses of homes.
optionExplanations: Correct. Covenants can limit building size or design.
Foreclosure is a lender remedy, not a covenant function.
Taxes are set by government, not subdivision covenants.
Covenants do not transfer ownership.

questions.q: A right-of-way allowing a neighbor to cross your land is a type of:
questions.explain: An easement appurtenant benefits one parcel of land and burdens another, such as a right-of-way for access.
optionExplanations: Correct. A right-of-way between neighboring parcels is commonly an easement appurtenant.
An encroachment is an unauthorized physical intrusion.
Eminent domain is a governmental taking power.
A leasehold is a tenant's interest under a lease.

questions.q: Which federal law prohibits discrimination in housing based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin, disability, or familial status?
questions.explain: The Fair Housing Act bans discrimination in most housing-related transactions based on protected characteristics.
optionExplanations: The Fair Credit Reporting Act regulates consumer credit reporting.
Correct. The Fair Housing Act prohibits housing discrimination.
The Sherman Antitrust Act addresses monopolistic business practices.
Truth in Lending requires disclosure of credit terms.

questions.q: A buyer who puts down less than 20% on a home may be required to pay:
questions.explain: Lenders often require PMI on conventional loans with a down payment below 20 percent to reduce default risk.
optionExplanations: Property tax is owed regardless of down payment size.
Correct. PMI is commonly required when equity is below 20 percent.
Appraisal fees are separate one-time valuation costs.
Origination fees are lender charges, not specifically tied to the 20 percent threshold.

questions.q: The process of determining a buyer's ability to qualify for a mortgage is:
questions.explain: Underwriting is the lender's review of income, assets, credit, debt, and the property to decide whether to approve the loan.
optionExplanations: An appraisal estimates property value.
Correct. Underwriting evaluates loan eligibility and risk.
Escrow holds funds and documents.
Recording places documents in the public record.

questions.q: In a fixed-rate mortgage:
options: Payments fluctuate based on interest rates
Interest rate stays the same for the loan term
Payments start low and increase over time
questions.explain: A fixed-rate mortgage keeps the note rate constant for the entire term, making principal-and-interest payments predictable.
optionExplanations: Adjustable-rate loans fluctuate with interest changes.
Principal amortization changes over time, so this statement is inaccurate.
Correct. The interest rate remains unchanged for the term.
That describes certain graduated or adjustable structures, not a fixed-rate loan.

questions.q: A balloon mortgage:
options: Requires interest-only payments until the loan ends
Requires one large payment at the end of the term
questions.explain: A balloon mortgage has a remaining balance due in one large lump-sum payment at maturity.
optionExplanations: Some balloon loans may have periodic payments, so interest-only is not always required.
Annual rate increases are not what defines a balloon loan.
Correct. The key feature is the large final payment.
A fully amortized loan pays off by the end without a balloon.

questions.q: A buyer chooses an adjustable-rate mortgage (ARM). Which risk do they face?
questions.explain: With an ARM, the interest rate can reset according to market indexes, which can raise future payments.
optionExplanations: Lower initial payments may be a feature, not the risk.
Correct. Rates can change, increasing payment uncertainty.
An ARM does not keep a fixed rate for the full term.
There is definitely risk that payments may increase.

questions.q: Escrow is used to:
options: Hold funds and documents until contract terms are met
questions.explain: Escrow is a neutral holding arrangement for money and documents until all terms of the transaction are satisfied.
optionExplanations: Escrow does not simply mean paying the seller directly.
Correct. Escrow holds items until the deal conditions are fulfilled.
Escrow does not eliminate taxes.
Mortgage insurance is not transferred through escrow as its main purpose.

questions.q: The closing disclosure must be given to the buyer:
questions.explain: Under federal mortgage disclosure rules, borrowers generally must receive the Closing Disclosure at least 3 business days before consummation.
optionExplanations: Receiving it at signing would be too late under the standard rule.
Correct. It must generally be provided at least 3 business days before closing.
After closing would not satisfy disclosure timing requirements.
Application triggers the Loan Estimate, not the Closing Disclosure.

questions.q: Which government agency insures FHA loans?
options: Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
questions.explain: FHA is part of HUD, and HUD administers the program that insures FHA-approved mortgage loans.
optionExplanations: The FHFA regulates entities like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.
The Federal Reserve does not insure FHA loans.
Correct. HUD oversees FHA mortgage insurance.
The CFPB enforces consumer finance rules but does not insure FHA loans.

questions.q: If a buyer defaults on their mortgage, the lender may initiate:
questions.explain: Foreclosure is the legal process a lender uses to recover the debt after mortgage default.
optionExplanations: Eminent domain is a government taking power.
Correct. Mortgage default can lead to foreclosure.
Refinancing replaces a loan and is not the lender's legal remedy after default.
Tax sales arise from delinquent property taxes, not directly from mortgage default.

questions.q: A legally binding agreement between two or more parties is a:
questions.explain: A contract is an enforceable agreement creating legal obligations between the parties.
optionExplanations: Correct. A contract is a legally binding agreement.
A leasehold is an interest in leased property.
A deed transfers title to real estate.
A lien is a security claim against property.

questions.q: A contract where one party agrees to buy only if certain conditions are met contains:
questions.explain: A contingency clause makes the obligation dependent on specific events, such as financing approval or inspection results.
optionExplanations: A warranty clause concerns promises about facts or quality.
Correct. A contingency clause conditions performance on stated events.
An escrow clause addresses handling of funds or documents.
A habendum clause is associated with deeds, not purchase contingencies.

questions.q: If both parties agree to cancel a contract before performance, it is called:
questions.explain: Mutual rescission occurs when both parties agree to terminate the contract and release each other from future duties.
optionExplanations: Novation substitutes a new party or obligation.
Correct. Mutual rescission is agreed cancellation.
Breach is an unexcused failure to perform.
Assignment transfers rights, not necessarily the whole cancellation of duties.

questions.q: A contract signed under threat is:
questions.explain: A contract entered into under duress is usually voidable at the option of the party subjected to the threat.
optionExplanations: Correct. Duress generally makes a contract voidable.
Void means it never had legal effect at all.
A contract under threat is not properly enforceable against the harmed party.
Executory only means performance is still owed by one or both parties.

questions.q: If a seller backs out after signing, the buyer may seek:
questions.explain: Because real property is considered unique, a buyer may ask a court for specific performance compelling the seller to complete the sale.
optionExplanations: Correct. Specific performance is a common remedy in real estate because each property is unique.
Mutual rescission requires both parties' agreement, not unilateral seller refusal.
A lease option is a transaction structure, not a remedy.
A contingency release is not the standard court remedy for seller breach.

questions.q: An oral contract for the sale of real estate is generally:
options: Void under the Statute of Frauds
questions.explain: Real estate sale contracts are generally required to be in writing under the Statute of Frauds to be enforceable.
optionExplanations: Oral agreements for sale are generally not enforceable.
Correct. The Statute of Frauds generally requires writing.
Witnessing alone usually does not satisfy the writing requirement.
The general rule is written enforceability, not broad oral permission.

questions.q: An option contract gives the buyer:
options: Right but not obligation to buy
questions.explain: An option contract grants the buyer the right to purchase within a set time, but the buyer is not required to exercise it.
optionExplanations: An option does not force the buyer to purchase.
Correct. It grants a right without an obligation.
Ownership does not transfer until the option is exercised and conveyed.
An option is not itself a leasehold interest.

questions.q: The most common appraisal method for single-family homes is the:
questions.explain: Single-family residences are most often valued by comparing them with recently sold similar homes in the market.
optionExplanations: The cost approach is often used for newer or special-purpose properties.
The income approach is more common for income-producing property.
Correct. The sales comparison approach is standard for owner-occupied homes.
Capitalization is a method associated with income valuation.

questions.q: A property's net operating income divided by its value is the:
questions.explain: The capitalization rate equals net operating income divided by value or price and is used to analyze income property returns.
optionExplanations: Loan-to-value compares loan amount to value.
Gross rent multiplier uses gross rent, not NOI.
Correct. NOI divided by value is the capitalization rate.
Equity percentage is ownership interest, not the ratio described.

questions.q: Which principle of value says that a property's value is influenced by the cost of acquiring a similar property?
questions.explain: The principle of substitution says a rational buyer will not pay more for one property than the cost of obtaining a comparable substitute.
optionExplanations: Contribution measures how much one feature adds to total value.
Correct. Substitution compares the property to similar alternatives.
Anticipation concerns future benefits.
Competition concerns market rivalry, not the appraisal principle here.

questions.q: In appraisal, functional obsolescence might be
questions.explain: Functional obsolescence is a loss in value caused by outdated design, layout, or features within the property itself.
optionExplanations: A leaky roof is a physical condition issue.
Correct. An outdated kitchen is an internal design deficiency.
A new roof is an improvement, not obsolescence.
Rising taxes are an external economic factor.

questions.q: The cost approach to valuation is most often used for:
questions.explain: The cost approach is especially useful for newer improvements because replacement cost and depreciation can be estimated more reliably.
optionExplanations: Correct. New construction is a common setting for the cost approach.
Older neighborhoods are more often valued with sales comparison.
Agricultural land is not the classic best use of the cost approach.
Rental properties are commonly analyzed with the income approach.

questions.q: A comparable property sold for $200,000 but had a two-car garage while the subject property has a one-car garage. The appraiser will:
options: Add value to the subject property
questions.explain: Because the comparable is superior, the appraiser adjusts the comparable downward to make it more like the subject property.
optionExplanations: A superior comparable is not adjusted upward.
Correct. The appraiser subtracts value from the comparable.
Adjustments are made to comparables, not directly to the subject.
A garage difference usually requires an adjustment.

questions.q: A property manager's primary responsibility is to:
questions.explain: Property managers aim to operate the property efficiently, maintain occupancy, and maximize net operating income for the owner.
optionExplanations: Higher turnover usually increases costs and vacancy loss.
Correct. Maximizing net income is a core property management objective.
Property taxes are set by taxing authorities, not directly by the manager.
Mortgage rates are set by lenders and markets, not property managers.

questions.q: If a tenant fails to pay rent, the property manager's first step is usually:
options: Serve a notice to pay or quit
questions.explain: The usual first legal step is written notice requiring payment or surrender before filing an eviction action.
optionExplanations: Eviction is usually filed after required notice.
Correct. A pay-or-quit notice is the common first step.
Changing locks without process can be illegal self-help eviction.
Police are generally not the first step for ordinary nonpayment.

questions.q: Preventive maintenance is:
questions.explain: Preventive maintenance consists of scheduled inspections and routine service intended to reduce breakdowns and larger repair costs.
optionExplanations: That is reactive maintenance.
Correct. Preventive maintenance is regular upkeep before failure occurs.
Remodeling for profit is capital improvement, not preventive maintenance.
It includes more than only tenant-requested repairs.

questions.q: Which type of lease bases rent on a percentage of the tenant's sales?
questions.explain: A percentage lease requires the tenant to pay base rent plus a percentage of business sales, common in retail properties.
optionExplanations: A gross lease typically includes many expenses in one rent payment.
A net lease shifts certain expenses to the tenant but is not defined by sales volume.
Correct. Percentage leases tie part of rent to sales.
A ground lease concerns land use over a long period.

questions.q: In property management, CAM charges refer to:
questions.explain: CAM stands for common area maintenance, covering shared-space costs such as hallways, parking lots, landscaping, and lighting.
optionExplanations: Correct. CAM means common area maintenance.
This is not the real estate meaning of CAM in leases.
This is not the standard leasing term.
This is broader management language, not the specific lease charge acronym.

questions.q: What best describes a fee simple estate?
options: The highest form of private ownership interest
A leasehold limited to one year
Ownership that ends automatically at closing
questions.explain: Fee simple estate is the broadest private ownership interest, with potentially indefinite duration and full bundle of rights, subject to government powers.
optionExplanations: Temporary possession is more like a leasehold.
Correct. Fee simple is the broadest private ownership form.
A one-year term is not fee simple.
Closing does not automatically terminate fee simple ownership.

questions.q: Which item is most likely to be considered a trade fixture?
options: A tenant's removable store display shelving
questions.explain: A trade fixture is an item installed by a commercial tenant for business use that can usually be removed before the lease ends if damage is repaired.
optionExplanations: Correct. Removable business shelving is a classic trade fixture.
Central air is typically a building fixture.
A fireplace is permanently attached real property.
A staircase is part of the structure and not a trade fixture.

questions.q: Which type of ownership allows two or more people to own equal interests with a right of survivorship?
questions.explain: Joint tenancy includes the right of survivorship, meaning a deceased owner's interest passes automatically to the surviving joint tenants.
optionExplanations: Tenancy in common does not automatically include survivorship.
Correct. Joint tenancy includes survivorship.
Severalty means sole ownership by one person.
Leasehold is a tenant's possessory interest, not co-ownership.

questions.q: In a tenancy in common, what happens when one owner dies?
options: Their interest automatically passes to the other co-owners
Their interest passes according to their will or state law
The property reverts to the landlord
questions.explain: A tenancy in common has no right of survivorship, so the deceased owner's share passes through their estate.
optionExplanations: Automatic transfer to co-owners describes joint tenancy.
Correct. The share passes by will or inheritance law.
There is no landlord in a tenancy in common ownership interest.
The ownership interests do not disappear.

questions.q: A life estate is ownership that lasts for:
options: The life of a named person
As long as property taxes are paid
questions.explain: A life estate is measured by the life of a designated person, after which ownership passes to another party.
optionExplanations: Correct. A life estate ends at the death of the measuring life.
A life estate is not defined by a fixed 99-year term.
Taxes do not determine the estate's duration.
Refinancing does not define a life estate.

questions.q: Which government power allows property to return to the state when an owner dies without heirs and no valid will?
questions.explain: Escheat is the state's power to take title when a person dies intestate and without legal heirs.
optionExplanations: Police power regulates land use and public welfare.
Correct. Escheat transfers ownerless property to the state.
Eminent domain involves taking for public use with compensation.
Foreclosure follows loan default.

questions.q: A mechanic's lien usually arises when:
options: A tenant refuses to renew a lease
A contractor is not paid for labor or materials
A borrower misses one mortgage payment
questions.explain: A mechanic's lien protects contractors or suppliers who improve property but are not paid for the work or materials provided.
optionExplanations: Lease renewal issues do not create mechanic's liens.
Correct. Unpaid construction work can produce a mechanic's lien.
Tax delinquency creates tax liens.
Mortgage delinquency may lead to foreclosure, not a mechanic's lien.

questions.q: Which document is commonly used to remove a possible cloud on title when no ownership warranties are being made?
questions.explain: A quitclaim deed transfers whatever interest the grantor may have without guaranteeing valid title, so it is often used to clear minor title issues among related parties.
optionExplanations: A general warranty deed provides title warranties instead.
Correct. Quitclaim deeds make no promises about title quality.
A mortgage note is a promise to repay a loan.
A Closing Disclosure summarizes loan and settlement costs.

questions.q: Title insurance primarily protects against:
options: Hidden defects in title existing before policy issuance
Normal wear and tear on the structure
questions.explain: Title insurance covers losses from covered title defects such as undisclosed liens, recording errors, or ownership claims that existed before the policy date.
optionExplanations: Tax increases are not the main risk insured by title insurance.
Correct. It protects against covered pre-existing title defects.
Structural deterioration is handled by property insurance or maintenance.
Interest-rate changes are financial market risks, not title risks.

questions.q: Which description best fits an easement in gross?
options: It benefits a neighboring parcel of land
It benefits a person or company rather than a parcel
It transfers full ownership of a strip of land
It always expires after one year
questions.explain: An easement in gross benefits an individual or entity, such as a utility company, rather than another parcel of land.
optionExplanations: That describes an easement appurtenant.
Correct. Utilities often hold easements in gross.
An easement grants use rights, not full ownership.
Its duration depends on the grant, not a fixed one-year rule.

questions.q: Which statement about zoning variances is most accurate?
options: They permanently eliminate all zoning laws in an area
They allow a property owner relief from specific zoning requirements
They transfer title to the local government
They are the same as restrictive covenants
questions.explain: A variance is permission from a zoning authority to deviate from a specific zoning rule when strict enforcement would create hardship.
optionExplanations: A variance does not cancel zoning for an entire area.
Correct. It grants limited relief from specific rules.
A variance does not transfer ownership.
Restrictive covenants are private restrictions, not municipal zoning relief.

questions.q: Building codes are primarily designed to:
options: Protect health and safety in construction
questions.explain: Building codes establish minimum construction standards intended to protect occupants and the public from unsafe conditions.
optionExplanations: Interest rates are set by lenders and markets.
Correct. Codes focus on safety and structural standards.
Codes may affect value indirectly but do not determine it.
No law can guarantee appreciation.

questions.q: Which of the following would most likely create functional obsolescence?
options: A four-bedroom house with only one bathroom
A rise in unemployment in the city
questions.explain: Functional obsolescence comes from a design or utility deficiency within the property, such as an outdated or inconvenient floor plan.
optionExplanations: Nearby factory noise is external economic obsolescence.
A cracked driveway is physical deterioration.
Correct. Too few bathrooms for the house size is a functional deficiency.
Rising unemployment is an external economic factor.

questions.q: External obsolescence is best illustrated by:
options: A major employer leaving the area
questions.explain: External obsolescence results from outside forces beyond the owner's control, such as neighborhood decline or adverse economic changes.
optionExplanations: Worn carpet is physical deterioration.
An outdated furnace is internal and functional or physical depending on context.
Correct. Loss of a major employer can depress nearby values.
A leaky sink is a physical maintenance issue.

questions.q: Which loan term refers to the amount borrowed compared with the value of the property?
questions.explain: The loan-to-value ratio compares the mortgage amount to the appraised value or purchase price used by the lender.
optionExplanations: Debt-to-income compares borrower obligations with income.
Capitalization rate measures return on income property.
Correct. LTV compares the loan to property value.
Gross rent multiplier uses price and gross rent, not loan amount.

questions.q: A buyer who pays discount points at closing is usually trying to:
options: Reduce the interest rate on the loan
Eliminate the need for an appraisal
questions.explain: Discount points are prepaid interest paid to lower the note rate on the mortgage.
optionExplanations: Correct. Points are commonly used to buy down the rate.
Appraisals are separate underwriting requirements.
Title insurance is unrelated to rate buydowns.
Paying points usually costs cash; it does not aim to increase the balance.

questions.q: Which mortgage clause allows a lender to demand full repayment if the property is sold without the lender's consent?
questions.explain: A due-on-sale clause lets the lender call the entire remaining balance due if ownership is transferred without approval.
optionExplanations: A prepayment clause addresses early payoff terms.
An acceleration clause is commonly triggered by default, not simply sale.
Correct. Due-on-sale applies when the property is transferred.
Defeasance describes release of the lien when the debt is fully paid.

questions.q: Amortization refers to:
options: The gradual repayment of a loan through periodic installments
The increase in property value over time
The transfer of possession to a tenant
questions.explain: Amortization is the process of paying off debt over time through scheduled payments of principal and interest.
optionExplanations: Correct. Loan balances are reduced through periodic amortized payments.
That describes appreciation, not amortization.
Recording is the public filing of documents.
Tenant possession is a leasing concept.

questions.q: Which expense is typically prorated at closing?
options: Property taxes already accrued for part of the year
The original construction cost of the home
questions.explain: Items such as property taxes, rent, and association dues are often prorated so buyer and seller each pay their fair share for the period of ownership.
optionExplanations: Remodeling costs are not standard prorated settlement items.
Correct. Accrued property taxes are commonly prorated.
Furniture is personal property and not a standard closing proration.
Original construction cost is irrelevant to current closing proration.

questions.q: Earnest money is best described as:
options: A lender fee charged at closing
A buyer's good-faith deposit showing serious intent
A government tax on deed transfers
A monthly payment into escrow for repairs
questions.explain: Earnest money is a deposit made with an offer to demonstrate the buyer's seriousness and provide security under the contract terms.
optionExplanations: It is not simply a lender fee.
Correct. Earnest money shows good faith and can be applied at closing.
Transfer tax is a separate government charge.
Repair escrows are different from earnest money deposits.

questions.q: Which contract provision lets a buyer cancel if the home inspection reveals unacceptable defects?
questions.explain: An inspection contingency gives the buyer the right to renegotiate or withdraw if inspection findings are unsatisfactory under the contract terms.
optionExplanations: A habendum clause appears in deeds, not purchase inspections.
Acceleration concerns loan default.
Correct. Inspection contingencies protect buyers from major undisclosed defects.
An escalation clause addresses competing offer prices.

questions.q: Which element is generally required for a valid contract?
questions.explain: Consideration is something of legal value exchanged between the parties and is a basic requirement for an enforceable contract.
optionExplanations: Recording a deed is not required to form every contract.
Correct. Contracts generally require consideration.
A mortgage broker is optional and not a legal element.
Title insurance is unrelated to contract formation.

questions.q: Which lease gives the tenant possession for a fixed start and end date?
questions.explain: An estate for years lasts for a definite term stated in the lease, such as one year or six months.
optionExplanations: Correct. A fixed beginning and ending date creates an estate for years.
A periodic tenancy automatically renews for successive periods.
Tenancy at will has no fixed term and continues by consent.
Tenancy at sufferance arises from holding over after expiration.

questions.q: Which occupancy item is most important when a lender orders an appraisal for a conventional mortgage?
options: The market value of the property
questions.explain: The lender relies on the appraisal to estimate market value so the collateral is sufficient for the proposed loan amount.
optionExplanations: Cosmetic preference is irrelevant to underwriting.
Correct. The appraisal's main purpose is to estimate market value.
Moving timing is not the primary appraisal issue.
Title company location does not affect value.

questions.q: The gross rent multiplier is most useful for comparing:
options: Single-family homes with no rental market
Income properties using price and gross rent
questions.explain: The gross rent multiplier compares property price to gross rental income and is used as a quick screening tool for income-producing property.
optionExplanations: GRM is not mainly for owner-occupied farms.
Without rental income, GRM is not useful.
Correct. GRM relates sale price to gross rent.
Vacant land has no gross rent stream in the usual GRM analysis.

questions.q: An appraiser would most likely use the income approach when valuing a:
options: Vacant residential lot for a custom home
questions.explain: Income-producing properties such as apartment buildings are commonly valued by converting expected income into value.
optionExplanations: Correct. Apartment buildings are classic income-approach properties.
Owner-occupied cottages are more often valued by sales comparison.
Schools are special-purpose properties and often use other methods.
Vacant residential lots are not typically valued by income capitalization.

questions.q: Which of the following best defines net operating income?
questions.explain: Net operating income equals effective gross income minus operating expenses, before deducting mortgage payments and income taxes.
optionExplanations: Debt service is not deducted to calculate NOI.
Correct. NOI is income after operating expenses but before financing costs.
Sale proceeds minus loan balance is equity, not NOI.
Depreciation is not added to gross rent to define NOI in appraisal usage.

questions.q: A landlord who pays all operating expenses while the tenant pays one flat rent is usually using a:
questions.explain: In a gross lease, the tenant pays a fixed rent and the landlord typically covers many operating expenses.
optionExplanations: A net lease shifts some expenses to the tenant.
A ground lease concerns land use, often long term.
Correct. Gross leases bundle many expenses into the rent.
A percentage lease includes rent tied to sales.

questions.q: A triple-net lease generally requires the tenant to pay:
options: Rent plus taxes, insurance, and maintenance
No rent if sales are low
questions.explain: Under a triple-net lease, the tenant usually pays base rent plus property taxes, insurance, and maintenance or common-area expenses.
optionExplanations: Utilities alone do not define a triple-net lease.
Correct. NNN leases shift taxes, insurance, and maintenance to the tenant.
CAM may be included, but taxes and insurance are also key.
Sales level does not eliminate rent in a triple-net lease.

questions.q: Which action by a property manager would most directly reduce vacancy loss?
options: Speeding unit turnover and marketing vacant space
questions.explain: Fast turnover, prompt repairs, and active marketing help return vacant units to paying occupancy more quickly.
optionExplanations: Ignoring complaints can increase turnover and vacancy.
Correct. Faster turnover and marketing reduce lost rent time.
Overpricing usually increases vacancy.
Delaying repairs can make units harder to lease.

questions.q: Security deposits are primarily intended to protect the landlord against:
options: Unpaid obligations or damage beyond ordinary wear
questions.explain: Security deposits help cover unpaid rent, excessive cleaning, or damages beyond normal wear and tear, subject to state law.
optionExplanations: Appreciation is unrelated to deposits.
Correct. Deposits protect against unpaid rent and abnormal damage.
Zoning changes are government actions, not deposit issues.
Interest rates do not determine deposit use.

questions.q: A lease clause that bars certain uses, such as operating a loud nightclub in a retail center, is most directly aimed at preserving:
questions.explain: Use restrictions in leases help maintain compatible occupancy, protect other tenants, and support quiet enjoyment of the premises.
optionExplanations: Correct. Use clauses preserve compatibility and tenant enjoyment.
Underwriting is a loan issue, not the main lease purpose here.
Title insurance is unrelated to tenant use restrictions.
Amortization schedules are loan terms, not lease-use controls.

questions.q: When a listing agreement gives one broker the exclusive right to market and earn a commission regardless of who finds the buyer, it is called an:
questions.explain: An exclusive-right-to-sell listing gives the broker the strongest protection because a commission is owed if the property sells during the listing term.
optionExplanations: Open listings allow multiple brokers and only pay the one who produces the buyer.
Correct. Exclusive-right-to-sell guarantees the broker commission if the property sells.
Net listings are based on seller proceeds and are restricted or disfavored in many places.
Option listing is not the standard term here.

questions.q: The person who lists property with a real estate broker is the:
questions.explain: In agency law, the client who authorizes the broker to act is the principal.
optionExplanations: A lessee is a tenant.
A grantor conveys an interest by deed.
Correct. The client is the principal.
An appraiser values property and is not the listing client.

questions.q: A broker acting only for the seller owes the buyer:
options: The duty to negotiate the lowest possible price
questions.explain: Even without representing the buyer, a seller's broker generally owes the buyer honest dealing and disclosure of material facts as required by law.
optionExplanations: Obedience is owed to the agent's principal, not the opposing party.
Full loyalty is not owed to the nonclient buyer.
Correct. Honesty and required disclosures are still owed.
Negotiating the lowest price would conflict with representing the seller.

questions.q: Which practice is illegal under federal fair housing law?
options: Refusing to rent because the applicant has children
Requiring all applicants to complete the same written application
Advertising a property by square footage and amenities
questions.explain: Refusing to rent because an applicant has children is discrimination based on familial status, a protected class under the Fair Housing Act.
optionExplanations: Correct. Familial-status discrimination is prohibited in most housing transactions.
Uniform procedures applied equally are generally lawful.
Income verification can be lawful if applied consistently.
Describing the property itself is generally permissible.

questions.q: Steering in real estate refers to:
options: Encouraging buyers toward or away from areas based on protected characteristics
Showing only the highest-priced homes first
questions.explain: Steering is the unlawful practice of channeling buyers or renters into or away from neighborhoods based on protected classes.
optionExplanations: Correct. Steering is discriminatory guidance based on protected traits.
Comparing lenders is not steering.
Showing expensive homes may be poor service but is not the specific fair-housing term.
Traffic discussion alone is not steering.

questions.q: Redlining is best described as:
options: Charging the same rate to all borrowers
Refusing or limiting lending in certain areas based on discriminatory criteria
questions.explain: Redlining is the discriminatory practice of denying or restricting credit in certain neighborhoods based on prohibited considerations rather than legitimate underwriting.
optionExplanations: Uniform pricing is the opposite of discriminatory line-drawing.
Correct. Redlining restricts credit based on discriminatory area treatment.
Electronic recording is unrelated.
A low sale price is not redlining.

questions.q: A seller who intentionally conceals a known foundation defect is most likely committing:
questions.explain: Knowingly hiding a material defect can constitute fraudulent concealment because the buyer is deprived of important information affecting value and safety.
optionExplanations: Puffing is exaggerated opinion, not concealment of a material defect.
Correct. Intentional concealment of a major defect is fraud.
Specific performance is a contract remedy.
Subrogation is an insurance principle.

questions.q: Which of the following is the best example of puffing rather than misrepresentation?
options: Claiming there are no roof leaks when the seller knows there are
Saying a home has the most charming view in town
Falsifying the square footage on a listing
Hiding termite damage behind new paneling
questions.explain: Puffing is a subjective sales opinion that a reasonable buyer would not treat as a precise factual guarantee.
optionExplanations: False statements about leaks are factual misrepresentations.
Correct. Calling a view charming is subjective opinion.
Falsifying square footage is a factual misstatement.
Concealing termite damage is fraudulent concealment.

questions.q: Which event usually terminates a listing agreement?
options: The property owner's change of paint color
questions.explain: Listing agreements commonly end when the stated contract term expires, unless renewed or terminated earlier under contract terms.
optionExplanations: Neighborhood opinions do not end the contract.
Correct. Expiration is a standard termination event.
Paint color changes do not terminate the agreement.
An appraisal visit has no effect on listing validity.

questions.q: Which duty requires an agent to place the client's interests above the agent's own?
questions.explain: Loyalty is a core fiduciary duty requiring the agent to act in the principal's best interests.
optionExplanations: Correct. Loyalty means prioritizing the client's interests.
Recording is a public records act.
Substitution is an appraisal principle.
Amortization is a loan repayment concept.

questions.q: A comparative market analysis is prepared primarily to:
options: Estimate a likely listing or sale price using recent comparables
Provide a legally binding appraisal for court
questions.explain: A CMA uses recent comparable sales and market data to help estimate an appropriate asking or offer price, but it is not a formal appraisal.
optionExplanations: Correct. A CMA helps estimate market price using comparables.
A CMA is not the same as a formal appraisal in legal settings.
Title searches address ownership issues, not pricing.
Tax depreciation schedules are a different task.

questions.q: Which statement best describes equity in a home?
options: The amount the owner owes in property tax
The owner's value interest after debts against the property
questions.explain: Equity is the difference between the property's market value and the debt secured by it.
optionExplanations: Property tax owed is not equity.
Correct. Equity is value minus liens or debt.
Down payment contributes to equity but is not the entire concept.
Insurance premiums are operating costs, not ownership equity.

questions.q: Refinancing a mortgage usually means:
options: Replacing an existing loan with a new loan
questions.explain: Refinancing pays off the existing mortgage and replaces it with a new loan, often to change the rate, term, or cash position.
optionExplanations: Correct. Refinancing substitutes a new loan for the old one.
Title insurance is not permanently cancelled by refinancing.
Ownership does not transfer to the lender merely by refinancing.
Closing costs may still apply.

questions.q: A short sale occurs when:
options: A property is sold in less than 30 days
The lender accepts less than the amount owed on the mortgage
A lease is limited to six months
An auction is canceled before bidding begins
questions.explain: In a short sale, the lender agrees to release the lien even though sale proceeds are insufficient to pay the full loan balance.
optionExplanations: Marketing time does not define a short sale.
Correct. The lender takes less than the debt owed.
Lease length is unrelated.
Auction cancellation is not the definition.

questions.q: Which ownership form is held by a tenant under a lease?
questions.explain: A tenant has a leasehold estate, which gives the right to possess and use property for a limited period under the lease terms.
optionExplanations: Freehold estates involve ownership interests of indefinite or life duration.
Correct. A tenant's interest is leasehold.
Fee simple absolute is the highest ownership interest.
Joint tenancy is a co-ownership form, not tenancy under a rental lease.

questions.q: When a deed is recorded in the public records, the main purpose is to:
options: Give public notice of the transfer
questions.explain: Recording provides constructive notice to the public that an interest in the property has been transferred or encumbered.
optionExplanations: Recording cannot guarantee appreciation.
Correct. Recording gives public notice of ownership changes.
A separate instrument is required to create a mortgage.
Recording does not cure every existing title problem.

questions.q: Which of the following is most likely to be a capital improvement rather than a routine repair?
questions.explain: A capital improvement materially extends the property's life or adds value, while routine repairs simply maintain current condition.
optionExplanations: A lightbulb replacement is minor maintenance.
A small patch is an ordinary repair.
Correct. A new roof is a significant capital improvement.
Drain unclogging is routine maintenance.

questions.q: An owner who occupies one unit of a duplex and rents out the other unit is best described as owning:
questions.explain: A duplex with one rented unit still produces rental income and is commonly treated as a residential income property.
optionExplanations: A duplex is not only commercial property.
Mixed-use usually combines residential and commercial uses.
Correct. It is residential property that also generates income.
The property is improved, not vacant land.
